sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with a history of asthma symptoms, including coughing and wheezing, which occur frequently, approximately every month or every other month for the past six months.
She has been experiencing these symptoms despite using an albuterol inhaler, which she finds helpful, and has had to visit the urgent care and emergency department multiple times for exacerbations.
She has also had one hospitalization due to asthma at the age of 8 years.
The patient reports that her symptoms worsen when she gets sick, particularly with a cold, and notes that she has had a flare-up after visiting her aunt's house due to her cat.
She uses her albuterol inhaler almost daily, taking two puffs, and has had to use it at night a couple of times a week.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of asthma, which is her primary concern.
She does not have a history of smoking, vaping, or marijuana use.

She does not have any seasonal allergies and is not exposed to airborne particles in her daily activities, although she does occasionally go to bars with friends where she is exposed to secondhand smoke.
Review of systems: The patient reports no other symptoms outside of her asthma.
Current medications: Albuterol (Ventolin) 2 puffs as needed, prednisone (dosepak and taper pack) as prescribed by her primary care provider or urgent care.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 128/82, respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 99% on room air.

Physical exam: Diminished lung sounds throughout with occasional slight expiratory wheeze bilaterally, neck is supple, trachea is midline, no lymphadenopathy, no carotid bruit, strong bilateral pulses, no clubbing on fingertips, brisk capillary refill.
Test Results: Pulmonary function test results consistent with asthma.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has moderate intermittent asthma, which is consistent with her symptoms and pulmonary function test results.
Problem list: Asthma; Status: confirmed.
Plan:
I prescribed Flovent 110 micrograms one puff twice per day to be taken consistently to prevent daily symptoms and reduce the need for her albuterol inhaler.
She can continue to use her albuterol inhaler, two puffs every 4-6 hours as needed.
I will put together an asthma action plan to help manage her symptoms and identify triggers.
I will see her in a month to review her progress.
Follow-up: I will see the patient in a month to review her symptoms and adjust her treatment plan as needed.
